Summary: This study developed and validated a CT radiomics-based nomogram for distinguishing between T2 and T3 stages of laryngeal and hypopharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (LHSCC). The nomogram, incorporating a radiomic signature and morphological imaging characteristics, demonstrated good diagnostic performance and clinical usefulness for guiding treatment decisions.

Condition1.reason: The study involves manual delineation of regions of interest (ROIs) on CT images for feature extraction, indicating segmentation.

Condition2.reason: The segmentation was performed manually, so it is not fully automated.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts 851 radiomic features from CT images, which are hand-crafted features.

Condition4.reason: The study uses radiomic features in a CSV format, which is a tabular data format.

Condition5.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning approach. It relies on hand-crafted feature extraction and machine learning classifiers.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines.

Item2.reason: The study defines inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient enrollment, aiming for a representative study population.

Item3.reason: The reference standard is based on the AJCC TNM staging system, which is a well-established clinical standard.

Item4.reason: The study is a single-center retrospective study.

Item5.reason: The study uses contrast-enhanced CT images, which are a standard imaging technique for LHSCC assessment.

Item6.reason: The study provides details of the scanning parameters in Supplementary A1.

Item7.reason: The study specifies that histopathological examination was performed within 2 weeks after CT scanning, which is an appropriate interval.

Item8.reason: The study describes the delineation criteria in Supplementary A3.

Item9.reason: The segmentation was performed manually, so this item is not applicable.

Item10.reason: The test set segmentation masks were produced by two readers, not a single reader or automated tool.

Item11.reason: The study mentions standardization preprocessing of images and describes the image types used (original and wavelet transform features).

Item12.reason: The study uses PyRadiomics for feature extraction and mentions the version number.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types (original and wavelet transform features) and provides information on feature extraction parameters.

Item14.reason: The study assesses the reproducibility of extracted features using ICCs and only uses features with ICCs > 0.75.

Item15.reason: The study uses Pearson's correlation coefficient (PCC) for dimensionality reduction.

Item16.reason: The study uses multiple feature selection methods and cross-validation to ensure the appropriateness of dimensionality compared to data size.

Item17.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline, so this item is not applicable.

Item18.reason: The study splits the data into training and test sets at a ratio of 7:3. They also use SMOTE for data balancing.

Item19.reason: The study analyzes potential confounding factors such as scanner type and uses the Kruskal-Wallis test to remove features showing significant statistical differences among different scanners.

Item20.reason: The study reports AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity for model evaluation. ROC curves are also presented.

Item21.reason: The study includes 95% confidence intervals (CI) for AUC values.

Item22.reason: The study performs the Hosmer–Lemeshow goodness-of-fit test and plots a calibration curve to assess the calibration of the nomogram.

Item23.reason: The study does not compare the performance of the model with a uni-parametric imaging approach.

Item24.reason: The study incorporates a morphological imaging characteristic (suspicious thyroid cartilage invasion) evaluated by radiologists into the nomogram and compares the performance with the radiomics signature alone. Decision curve analysis is used to evaluate the clinical application value of the nomogram.

Item25.reason: The study does not compare the performance of the model with a simple or classical statistical model.

Item26.reason: The model is tested on an independent test set sampled from the same source as the training set.

Item27.reason: The study is a single-center study and does not involve external testing.

Item28.reason: The study does not mention any data being shared with the public.

Item29.reason: The study does not mention any code being shared with the public.

Item30.reason: The study does not mention the model being shared with the public.
